MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares PositionRank against a solid set of relevant baselines (Mihalcea & Tarau 2004; Wan & Xiao 2008; Hulth 2003; Hasan & Ng 2014) on three scholarly-paper datasets using standard metrics (MRR, precision/recall/F1), which indicates generally fair and appropriate comparisons. However, the summary indicates no reporting of hyperparameters, no mention of statistical significance testing or ablation isolating the positional bias, and no stated limitations or code release; these omissions weaken confidence in fairness and reproducibility. Given these relatively minor but important gaps, the comparisons are good overall but not fully comprehensive or fully auditable. | B(4): The paper compares against several appropriate and influential baselines (Mihalcea & Tarau 2004, Wan & Xiao 2008, Hulth 2003, Hasan & Ng 2014) across three standard scholarly-paper datasets and reports multiple metrics (MRR, P/R/F1), which matches my empirical priorities. It clearly contrasts PositionRank to standard PageRank variants and shows sizable gains, and the related-work coverage appears adequate. Minor gaps remain: code and hyperparameter details aren’t reported (hurting reproducibility) and it’s not explicit whether any prior methods ever match or exceed PositionRank under some conditions, so I can’t judge complete fairness of comparisons. Overall the empirical comparison is strong but missing a few reproducibility and clarity elements.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares PositionRank against a solid set of relevant baselines (Mihalcea & Tarau 2004; Wan & Xiao 2008; Hulth 2003; Hasan & Ng 2014) on three scholarly-paper datasets using standard metrics (MRR, precision/recall/F1), which indicates generally fair and appropriate comparisons. However, the summary indicates no reporting of hyperparameters, no mention of statistical significance testing or ablation isolating the positional bias, and no stated limitations or code release; these omissions weaken confidence in fairness and reproducibility. Given these relatively minor but important gaps, the comparisons are good overall but not fully comprehensive or fully auditable.

It clearly contrasts PositionRank to standard PageRank variants and shows sizable gains, and the related-work coverage appears adequate. Minor gaps remain: code and hyperparameter details aren’t reported (hurting reproducibility) and it’s not explicit whether any prior methods ever match or exceed PositionRank under some conditions, so I can’t judge complete fairness of comparisons. Overall the empirical comparison is strong but missing a few reproducibility and clarity elements.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is overall well organized (introduction → method → experiments → conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early; writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage adequate. Notation appears consistent and figures/tables/examples are effective, making the method (position-biased PageRank, candidate phrase patterns) easy to follow. Presentation suffers minor omissions that affect clarity for readers aiming to reproduce or scrutinize the work: hyperparameters are not reported, code availability is not mentioned, and no limitations are stated. From a theoretician’s perspective these gaps are presentation flaws rather than fundamental clarity failures, so I rate it a 4. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a logical flow from introduction through method and experiments, and the main contributions (PositionRank and position-biased PageRank) are stated early and repeatedly. Notation appears consistent and the methodology (graph construction, scoring, candidate selection) is explained clearly; figures/tables and evaluation metrics seem effective and informative. Minor presentation gaps include omitted hyperparameter reporting and no stated limitations or code release, which reduce reproducibility and practical clarity for deployment. Overall the writing quality is good with only small omissions that could be addressed.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is overall well organized (introduction → method → experiments → conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early; writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage adequate. Notation appears consistent and figures/tables/examples are effective, making the method (position-biased PageRank, candidate phrase patterns) easy to follow. Presentation suffers minor omissions that affect clarity for readers aiming to reproduce or scrutinize the work: hyperparameters are not reported, code availability is not mentioned, and no limitations are stated. From a theoretician’s perspective these gaps are presentation flaws rather than fundamental clarity failures, so I rate it a 4.

Notation appears consistent and the methodology (graph construction, scoring, candidate selection) is explained clearly; figures/tables and evaluation metrics seem effective and informative. Minor presentation gaps include omitted hyperparameter reporting and no stated limitations or code release, which reduce reproducibility and practical clarity for deployment. Overall the writing quality is good with only small omissions that could be addressed.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The experimental evaluation compares PositionRank to multiple established baselines across three scholarly-paper datasets and reports sizable gains (up to 29.09%), which adequately supports the central empirical claim. However, the paper omits ablation/sensitivity analyses (e.g., role of position bias, window size, phrase-length cap) and provides no discussion of failure modes or limitations, and hyperparameters/code are not reported—hindering reproducibility and mechanistic understanding. Related work coverage is described as adequate, but the lack of formal analysis or statistical significance testing further weakens confidence in the claims. | B(3): The paper evaluates PositionRank on three relevant research-paper datasets and compares against multiple strong baselines using standard metrics (MRR, precision/recall/F1), which supports the core performance claims. However, it lacks reported hyperparameters, ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of position bias, window size), no discussion of failure cases or limitations, and code/reproducibility details are not mentioned—important for deployment and to fully validate robustness across settings.

reviewer_a.justification: The experimental evaluation compares PositionRank to multiple established baselines across three scholarly-paper datasets and reports sizable gains (up to 29.09%), which adequately supports the central empirical claim. However, the paper omits ablation/sensitivity analyses (e.g., role of position bias, window size, phrase-length cap) and provides no discussion of failure modes or limitations, and hyperparameters/code are not reported—hindering reproducibility and mechanistic understanding. Related work coverage is described as adequate, but the lack of formal analysis or statistical significance testing further weakens confidence in the claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates PositionRank on three relevant research-paper datasets and compares against multiple strong baselines using standard metrics (MRR, precision/recall/F1), which supports the core performance claims. However, it lacks reported hyperparameters, ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of position bias, window size), no discussion of failure cases or limitations, and code/reproducibility details are not mentioned—important for deployment and to fully validate robustness across settings.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the task is important and the paper delivers sizable empirical improvements using a simple, interpretable position-biased PageRank, with likely practical adoption. They also agree the contribution is incremental rather than transformative. Given the strong cross-dataset gains and ease of deployment, I award a 4 to reflect solid impact in practice despite limited theoretical novelty.

reviewer_a.justification: Extracting keyphrases from scholarly documents is an important practical problem and showing up to 29.09% improvement over strong baselines on multiple research-paper datasets (ACM KDD, WWW, Nguyen & Kan) indicates a useful empirical advance. However, the contribution appears to be an incremental, application-focused improvement (a position-biased PageRank and standard phrase aggregation) rather than a conceptual shift that would reshape how researchers think about the task. Given the lack of stated limitations, code, or hyperparameter reporting in the summary, the work is likely to be adopted by practitioners but is unlikely to open major new theoretical directions beyond improving unsupervised ranking-based extraction.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important, practical problem—unsupervised keyphrase extraction for scholarly documents—using a lightweight, interpretable method (position-biased PageRank) that is easy to implement and deploy in search/indexing pipelines. Empirically it reports sizable gains (up to 29.09%) over standard PageRank and several strong baselines across multiple research-paper datasets (ACM KDD, WWW, Nguyen & Kan), which suggests the idea of incorporating positional signals is broadly useful. While the contribution is incremental rather than transformative, its simplicity, cross-dataset validation, and strong empirical improvements make it likely to be adopted and extended in both research and production systems. Broader implications are moderate: the core idea of position-aware ranking can transfer to other extraction and ranking tasks, but the work is primarily focused on the keyphrase extraction subfield.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper presents a practical, interpretable extension of PageRank that injects position information and yields substantial empirical gains, but it is an incremental improvement rather than a fundamentally new theoretical contribution. Given the lack of formal guarantees and the contribution being a principled heuristic with clear utility, a moderate originality score is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a position-biased variant of PageRank that aggregates information from all occurrence positions of words and applies it to keyphrase extraction, which is a clear modification of an existing paradigm rather than a wholly new formulation. The problem (unsupervised keyphrase extraction) is well-studied and the authors compare against established PageRank-based baselines (Mihalcea & Tarau 2004) and other prior methods, so the contribution is an incremental but practical extension. From a theoretical-purist standpoint the method lacks formal analysis or novel guarantees and reads as a principled heuristic improvement; thus it informs experts but is not a fundamentally new idea. The reported empirical gains (up to ~29%) demonstrate usefulness but do not, by themselves, establish deep novelty.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a clear, interpretable modification to graph-based keyphrase extraction—injecting position information from all occurrences into a PageRank bias—which is a notable and practical extension of prior PageRank approaches. The problem is well-studied, so the contribution is not fundamentally new, but the technique is a non-trivial, easily implementable variant that yields substantial multi-dataset gains (up to 29%) against strong baselines, which would be informative to practitioners. The authors also differentiate their method from standard PageRank models and prior keyphrase systems in experiment design and evaluation.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses a core NLP task (unsupervised keyphrase extraction) using methods and evaluations that clearly fit a top NLP/ML venue and would interest the community. Minor concerns about theoretical analysis and full reproducibility details affect strength rather than topical fit, so the submission is nevertheless highly appropriate for the venue.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an NLP core task (unsupervised keyphrase extraction) using graph-based PageRank variants on scholarly documents, which squarely fits top NLP/ML venue topics and would interest the community working on information extraction and unsupervised methods. The use of standard research-paper datasets, established baselines, and common metrics (MRR, precision/recall/F1) indicates the experimental framing is aimed at this audience. Minor concerns remain about missing theoretical analysis and unreported hyperparameters/code (which the venue often values), but those affect strength rather than topical fit.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is a strong fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it addresses an important NLP task (keyphrase extraction) with a simple, interpretable, and deployable method (position-biased PageRank) and demonstrates substantial empirical gains (up to 29.09%) over strong baselines. Evaluation uses standard metrics (MRR, precision/recall/F1, performance@k) across multiple realistic scholarly datasets (ACM KDD, WWW from CiteSeerX, Nguyen & Kan), which matches the empirical rigor I value. While code and hyperparameter reporting are not mentioned in the summary (a minor reproducibility concern), the cross-dataset comparisons and clear baseline suite make it appropriate for the venue.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes clear, testable claims and provides convincing empirical evidence across multiple datasets that position-biased PageRank improves keyphrase extraction, and neither found logical flaws in the method. They also concur that the work lacks theoretical analysis, hyperparameter details, statistical significance testing, ablations isolating the positional effect, and reproducibility artifacts. Given the absence of fatal errors but notable methodological gaps, I score it a 4 to reflect solid empirical soundness tempered by missing rigor and reproducibility details.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents clear claims and experimental evidence (up to 29.09% improvements) showing PositionRank outperforms non-position-biased PageRank and several baselines on three research-paper datasets, so empirical support exists. However, there is no theoretical analysis of the position-biased PageRank (no convergence/behavioral guarantees), hyperparameters are not reported, statistical significance testing and ablation isolating the positional component are absent, and failure modes are unanalyzed. Given these methodological gaps, the results are promising but not sufficiently substantiated from a theoretical or experimental-rigor perspective.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper makes clear, testable claims and supports them with experiments across three real research-paper datasets and comparisons to strong baselines (standard PageRank variants and prior work), which aligns with my emphasis on empirical validation and lightweight, interpretable methods. Mathematical apparatus is straightforward (a position-biased PageRank) so no major derivation issues are evident, but the summary omits hyperparameter settings, statistical significance tests, and code release—limitations for reproducibility and assessment of robustness. I find no fatal logical gaps; results reported (up to 29% gains) plausibly justify the conclusions, though fuller experimental details and ablations would strengthen the technical case.

main_claims: Proposes PositionRank, an unsupervised keyphrase extraction model for scholarly documents.
Incorporates information from all positions of a word’s occurrences into a position-biased PageRank algorithm.
Outperforms PageRank models that do not account for word positions and other strong baselines, with improvements up to 29.09% on several research-paper datasets.
_summary.methodology: Builds an undirected word graph from nouns and adjectives co-occurring within a sliding window, applies a position-biased PageRank to score words, forms candidate phrases matching (adjective)*(noun)+ up to trigrams, and scores phrases by summing constituent word scores.
datasets: ACM KDD dataset (from CiteSeerX, via Gollapalli and Caragea 2014)
WWW dataset (from CiteSeerX, via Gollapalli and Caragea 2014)
Nguyen and Kan (2007) dataset (research papers from various disciplines)
baselines: Methods from prior work cited: Mihalcea and Tarau (2004); Wan and Xiao (2008); Hulth (2003); Hasan and Ng (2014)
_summary.topic_area: Keyphrase extraction / NLP for scholarly documents

reviewer_a.name: Theoretical Purist
reviewer_a.background: Senior NLP researcher with a PhD in computational linguistics and a track record of provable-methods papers; spends most reviewing bandwidth on algorithmic foundations and theoretical guarantees.
traits: penalises papers that present algorithmic changes without formal justification or theoretical analysis (e.g., convergence, optimality, or expected behavior under clear assumptions)
requires clear statistical significance testing and ablation isolating each claimed contribution; will deduct points if claims rely only on aggregate percentage improvements
penalises papers that do not analyze failure modes or provide qualitative/error analysis for representative cases
penalises empirical-only contributions that are incremental variations of existing algorithms unless accompanied by new theory or insight
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on whether the conceptual contribution is novel and principled, demanding formal/analytical support and careful ablation before granting high scores.

reviewer_b.name: Practical Empiricist
reviewer_b.background: Industry-affiliated senior NLP scientist with extensive experience deploying unsupervised and lightweight models for information extraction in production scholarly and enterprise search systems.
traits: rewards well-performing unsupervised or lightweight methods that improve practical metrics across multiple realistic datasets (gives >0.5 point bonus for consistent multi-dataset gains)
rewards methods emphasizing interpretability and simplicity (e.g., graph-based PageRank variants) that are easy to implement and integrate
rewards thorough empirical validation: cross-dataset evaluation, comparisons to strong baselines, and empirical ablations demonstrating contribution components
gives positive bias if authors release code/data or describe reproducible experimental setup in sufficient detail
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Values empirical effectiveness, robustness across datasets, and practical deployability; tolerates limited theory if empirical results are strong and reproducible.
